channel.title: Protections on Home
channel.description: Recent content in Protections on Home

item.title: Weapon_training_first_thoughts
item.description: I was showing this to some of my military buddies, and he brought up a pretty good point about it. The likelyhood of needing Griz to attack an intruder when I have a weapon is pretty low. Most likely the situation wouldn’t call for a weapon and a dog, and if it did, there’s going to be legal issues where we look at prison and a dead dog. So the idea of an intruder drill needs to be trained with this in mind. We need to think situations through and ensure that we respond in a defensive manner. This means we don’t send the dog on someone running away. We probably don’t want to send the dog initially. I think this shows how good IGP is, because what we want is a scary dog while we “talk” someone off the property.

item.title: Bark training 1
item.description: The goal is to give power to her barks. I want her to think she can make me come to her, or make me flee based on her barks. If I ask her to get scary, we want to see powerful barks. To get this we are going to use shaping and frustration with the tug. As she barks I’ll move closer. Initially, I should reward any vocal barks with forward motion and should avoid resetting. As time goes on I want to start getting stronger and more sustained barks. Eventually, this should turn into a game where I can move back or go still, and she’ll bring me to life with barking.

item.title: Bark training 2
item.description:
Alright this is like a week after Bark Training 1. We’ve done the tie back a few times and I think I can get her to do it with me holding her back. This can be confusing for her, and I wouldn’t want to try it if I didn’t think she was ready. And we’ll want to do this a few times before we move to having a helper.

The technique here is shaping again, this time instead of me getting closer to her, we get closer to the ball. I see some guys on youtube using agitation collars for this; but, I want to keep my leash pressure communication strong, so I’m going to use harnesses most of the time.
